question:
According to the introduction on Class AB power amplifiers, what is a key limitation of classical theory when predicting the linearity of an RFPA operated below Class A bias?

A. It assumes the RF device can switch instantaneously, leading to overly optimistic linearity predictions.

B. It overestimates efficiency by ignoring the heatsink cooling improvements in Class AB operation.

C. It incorrectly models the device conduction as a perfect linear amplifier regardless of bias point.

D. It neglects the nonlinearity caused by thermal effects in the device under high power conditions.

[Citation 1] (Source: sparse, Relevance: 0.119) /home/jw161/chatbot/rag_textbook_paper_pdf/textbook_pdf/Advanced Techniques in RF Power Amplifier Design - Steve C. Cripps.pdf:p17 :: 1 Class AB Amplifiers 1.1 Introduction The Class AB mode has been a focus for several generations of power ampli- fier designers, and for good reasons. It is a classical compromise, offering higher efficiency and cooler heatsinks than the linear and well-behaved Class A mode, but incurring some increased nonlinear effects which can be toler- ated, or even avoided, in some applications. The main goal in this chapter is to invite PA designers and device technologists to break out of the classical Class AB tunnel vision which seems to afflict a large proportion of their numbers. For too long, we have been assuming that our radio frequency power amplifier (RFPA) transistors obediently conduct precisely truncated sinewaves when the quiescent bias is reduced below the Class A point, regardless of the fact that an RF power device will typically have nowhere near the switching speed to perform the task with the assumed precision. The

A. CMOS PA Design The transconductance of a grid is utilized in the silicon CMOS amplifier’s principle of signal amplification; however, the low breakdown voltage of a silicon-based process limits the output power of a single-tube amplifier. An input matching circuit, a first-stage amplifying circuit, an inter-stage matching circuit, a second-stage amplifying circuit, and an output match- ing circuit are connected in order of importance in a V-band CMOS power amplifier. Class AB is chosen for the proposed design since it provides the best linearity ratios and efficiency for V-band applications. Class AB amplifiers, which have more efficiency than Class A amplifiers and better linearity than Class B amplifiers, are perfect for high-frequency applications that need to balance power efficiency and signal quality [12]. 1) Biasing Point: The choice of bias point is a crucial

This is a con- venient normalizing parameter, but in practice this amount of dissipation would take most higher power RF transistors well beyond their specified maximum rat - ings. 6 So the lower half, or even third, of the total plotted dissipation range will still represent a dissipation level which may cause some tens of degrees of temperature increase under steady state conditions. There appears to be a sensitive range of qui- escent settings in the deep Class AB region where the dissipation function can devi- ate rapidly from a flat characteristic. 7 It is still regarded as a matter of conjecture as to whether the linearity “sweet spot” which is frequently observed on the test bench is caused by this effect. question:
In designing an integrated microstrip transmission line to minimize loss while maintaining a high characteristic impedance (Z₀), which of the following design changes would be most effective according to the discussed trade-offs?

A. Increasing the signal line width significantly to reduce series resistance, accepting a decreased Z₀ and thus lower inductance per unit length and higher capacitance.

B. Decreasing the thickness of the signal line metal layer to reduce surface roughness, maintaining the same line width to keep Z₀ constant.

C. Increasing the spacing between the signal line and the ground plane dielectric layers to lower capacitance and increase Z₀ without changing the signal line width.

D. Using polycrystalline silicon for the signal line instead of metal layers to reduce loss by lowering sheet resistance.
